content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Larissa Seto 
Assistant City Attorney 
P. 0. Box 520 
123 Main Street 
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-177 
Dear Ms. Seto: 
October 5, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Ame Olson 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note 
that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under 
other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 
I 090. Moreover, we base this letter on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission 
does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Councilmember Olson from taking part 
in city council decisions regarding a project approximately 1,890 feet from his home that would 
include replacing an existing church with 27 new single-family homes and expanding an existing 
120-student private school to 298 students? 
CONCLUSION 
Based upon the facts provided, there does not appear to be a reasonably foreseeable material 
effect on the councilmember's interest in his residence. Councilmember Olson may take part in the 
decisions regarding the project. 
FACTS 
Councilmember Olson owns and lives in a residence on Chatham Court in the community 
known as the Ironwood neighborhood. Ironwood has a total of 193 single-family homes, senior 
multifamily housing, a church, and a private preschool. Councilmember Olson lives in a specific 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations, All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-15-177 
Page No. 2 
subsection of the Ironwood neighborhood known as The Village at Ironwood, a gated community 
for 55 and older adults with about 108 of the 193 homes. 
The proposed project is located at 3410-3450 Cornerstone Court and adjacent to Ironwood 
Drive and Busch Road. The project is approximately 1,890 feet from Councilmember Olson's 
home. More specifically, the project involves a general plan land use amendment, a zoning 
amendment, and a conditional use permit modification to eliminate the existing church, replace it 
with 27 new single-family homes, and expand the existing 120 student preschool into a 298 student 
school serving preschool to 6th grade. Ironwood Drive, via its intersection with Busch Road, is the 
only entrance and exit for the proposed project, The Village at Ironwood, and Councilmember 
Olson's home. 
The traffic analysis for this proposed project found that: 
• Replacing the existing church with 27 new homes will result in a decrease in a.m. peak trips 
from 25 to 20 trips. 
• Expanding the existing school will result in an increase of a.m. peak trips from 120 to 294. 
• The project will result in an increase of a.m. peak trips, at the intersection of Ironwood 
Drive and Busch Road, from 185 to 354. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official 
has a financial interest specified in Section 87103. A public official has a "financial interest" in a 
government decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 
87103.) Of the interests recognized under the Act, the only interest identified is Councilmember 
Olson's interest in real property resulting from his residence. (Section 87103(b).) Accordingly, we 
must determine whether the financial effect on Councilmember Olson's interest in his residence 
resulting from decisions regarding the project is both foreseeable and material. 
Foreseeability: 
For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in a decision (such as Councilmember 
Olson's residence), Regulation 18701(b) states: 
"A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If 
the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not 
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." 


Materiality: 
File No. A-15-177 
Page No. 3 
Recently revised Regulation l 8705.2(a) provides a list of circumstances under which the 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on real property in which an 
official has a financial interest is material. As relevant to your facts, the financial effect will be 
material if the decisions: 
"( I 0) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air 
quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of 
the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest. 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was 
of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market 
value of the official's property." 
In this case, the project is approximately 1,890 feet from Councilmember Olson's residence. 
However, the gated community in which he resides insulates Councilmember Olson's residence 
from the project. Considering the magnitude and location of the project, there is no indication that 
the project may have a measureable effect on parking, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality 
within the gated community. The one concerning element of the project is the fact that the 
Ironwood Drive, via its intersection with Busch Road, is the only entrance and exit for the proposed 
project, The Village at Ironwood, and Councilmember Olson's home. Accordingly, the pertinent 
issue is whether the foreseeable increase in traffic results in a material effect on Councilmember 
Olson's residence. 
As stated in the traffic analysis, the project will result in an increase of a.m. peak trips, at the 
intersection of Ironwood Drive and Busch Road, from 185 to 354. However, this appears a worst­
case scenario as the increase in traffic resulting from the expanded school would occur primarily at 
the beginning and the end of the school day. Moreover, the increase in traffic is slightly offset by 
the closing of the existing church, which would likely result in a decrease in weekend traffic, and 
the 27 additional single-family residences, which you have stated will result in a small decrease in 
traffic compared to the existing church. Based upon the facts provided, we find that it is unlikely 
that the relatively small foreseeable increase in traffic would influence the market value of the 
residence. Therefore, Councilmember Olson is not prohibited from taking part in the decisions. 


File No. A-15-177 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Councilmember Olson from taking part 
in city council decisions regarding a project approximately 1,890 feet from his home that would 
include replacing an existing church with 27 new single-family homes and expanding an existing 
120-student private school to 298 students?

effect on the councilmember's interest in his residence. Councilmember Olson may take part in the 
decisions regarding the project.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Councilmember Olson from taking part 
in city council decisions regarding a project approximately 1,890 feet from his home that would 
include replacing an existing church with 27 new single-family homes and expanding an existing 
120-student private school to 298 students?

CONCLUSION: Based upon the facts provided, there does not appear to be a reasonably foreseeable material 
effect on the councilmember's interest in his residence. Councilmember Olson may take part in the 
decisions regarding the project.
